gold_report: Train-of-four stimulations are done by delivering four successive supramaximal stimuli (at 500 ms intervals) to a peripheral nerve. Those groups of four stimulations are repeatedly delivered to evaluate the resulting pattern of muscular response. Normally, the resulting muscular responses (as measured via electromyography) should be equally strong (both within groups of four consecutive responses and over a succession of consecutive groups of such responses). If nondepolarizing neuromuscular blocking drugs (e.g., rocuronium) are administered, the amplitude of muscular responses decreases within a group of four consecutive responses (that the amplitude of the fourth response is significantly lower than that of the first one of the same group). This phenomenon is also referred to as “fade.” The effects of depolarizing neuromuscular blocking drugs (e.g., succinylcholine) are subdivided into two phases: In phase I, the drug binds to the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, inducing a single, persistent depolarization. Electromyographically, each of the individual responses in a group of four will be decreased by the same percentage, and the overall muscular responsiveness between consecutive groups of stimuli steadily decreases over time. If depolarizing neuromuscular agents are administered over a prolonged amount of time or a high dose is given, phase II may occur. In this phase, there is a fade in the muscular response, resembling the phenomenon seen in nondepolarizing agents. Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (e.g., neostigmine) potentiate the effects of depolarizing neuromuscular blocking drugs during phase I.

gold_report: (A) Aortic pressure is much greater than left ventricular pressure. Point A occurs right after atrial contraction (causing the first hump on the left atrial pressure curve; atrial kick) and marks the beginning of ventricular systole (isovolumetric contraction). (B) Opening of the aortic valve as the left ventricular pressure exceeds the aortic pressure. (B) to (C) Left ventricular contraction. Left ventricular and aortic pressures rise as the left ventricle ejects blood into the aorta. Pressures within the left ventricle and aorta are almost identical during this period. (C) Peak aortic and left ventricular pressures. After this point, the left ventricular and aortic pressures begin to decrease (reduced ejection phase). The aortic valve is still open during this phase. (D) Closure of the aortic valve as the left ventricular pressure falls below the aortic pressure. This marks the beginning of diastole (isovolumetric relaxation). (E) There is an increase in the aortic pressure (the dicrotic notch) that is caused by the closure of the aortic valve. Left ventricular pressure falls rapidly as the ventricles relax. Point E represents the highest pressure difference between the aorta and the left ventricle (aorta > left ventricle). It is responsible for the peak coronary blood flow at this phase. (F) The aortic pressure decreases gradually during ventricular relaxation. Due to its inherent vascular resistance, aortic pressures remain higher than left ventricular pressures during ventricular diastole.

3. Atrial contraction causes the first hump on the left atrial pressure curve.

6. The beginning of ventricular systole is isovolumetric contraction.
7. The aortic valve opens when the left ventricular pressure exceeds the aortic pressure.

9. Left ventricular and aortic pressures rise as the left ventricle ejects blood into the aorta.
10. During left ventricular contraction pressures within the left ventricle and aorta are almost identical.
11. Point C is when peak aortic and left ventricular pressures occur.
12. After Point C the left ventricular and aortic pressures begin to decrease.
13. This decrease in pressures is the reduced ejection phase.
14. The aortic valve is open during the reduced ejection phase.
15. The aortic valve closes when the left ventricular pressure falls below the aortic pressure.
16. The closure of the aortic valve marks the beginning of diastole.
17. The beginning of diastole is isovolumetric relaxation.
18. There is an increase in aortic pressure at Point E.
19. The increase in aortic pressure is called the dicrotic notch.
20. The closure of the aortic valve causes the dicrotic notch.
21. Left ventricular pressure falls rapidly as the ventricles relax.
22. Point E represents the highest pressure difference between the aorta and the left ventricle.
23. At Point E aortic pressure is greater than left ventricular pressure.

25. Aortic pressure decreases gradually during ventricular relaxation.
